1.matchText: Mongol-founded regime that had subjugated Goryeo in the thirteenth century
1.blurb: The Mongol Yuan dynasty's century of dominance over Korea did more than just install a tributary relationship. After bringing Goryeo to heel in the 1270s, the Yuan compelled Korean shipwrights and sailors to build and crew the invasion fleets that twice attempted to conquer Japan in 1274 and 1281. The Wihwado Retreat that founded Joseon was a direct response to the political vacuum the dying Yuan left behind — Korean elites debating whether to align with the new Ming or to recover the Manchurian territories that had drifted away during Mongol rule.

The legendary Gojoseon kingdom of mythological prehistory was the original 'Joseon' that Yi Seong-gye's new dynasty was deliberately invoking by name. The bear-into-a-woman Dangun foundation myth, the late Bronze Age Wiman conquest, and the Han commanderies of the second century BCE all belong to that earlier story — and the Joseon of 1392 was claiming a continuity with it that was more rhetorical than historical, but that resonated powerfully in Korean political memory.

The Aztec triple alliance was founded in 1428, almost exactly contemporary with the early Joseon consolidation. The two civilizations could not have been further apart culturally — one a Confucian bureaucratic monarchy in East Asia, the other a tribute-collecting warrior empire in central Mexico — but both were building durable state structures in the same generation. The early-modern world was being built simultaneously on every continent.

While Yi Seong-gye was founding Joseon in 1392, the Ottoman Empire under Bayezid I was at the height of its early Balkan expansion. The two dynasties would have parallel arcs across the next five centuries — both building durable bureaucratic states with strong religious-philosophical foundations, both eventually weakening in the eighteenth century, both struggling to modernize in the nineteenth century, both finally extinguished in the early twentieth century within twelve years of each other (Joseon in 1910, the Ottomans in 1922). The structural parallel between the two longest-lived early-modern Asian empires is striking.

2.matchText: the most sustained scientific patronage program in fifteenth-century East Asia
2.blurb: Sejong's Hall of Worthies was the same institutional pattern that the Han dynasty had pioneered with the Imperial Academy in 124 BCE — pay the scholars, give them a permanent home and access to the royal library, let them work on whatever the state needs scholarly work on. The Han version produced the Confucian state ideology that Joseon was now inheriting and applying. Every classical East Asian state that wanted to do scholarship at scale eventually figured out the same answer.

The House of Wisdom in Abbasid Baghdad under al-Ma'mun was the most ambitious state-funded research institute of the medieval world — an institution Sejong's Hall of Worthies consciously echoed in its smaller Korean fifteenth-century form. The Caliph's translators worked on Greek philosophy and Indian mathematics; Sejong's scholars worked on Korean phonology and astronomy. Different content, identical institutional logic: a wealthy state systematically converting royal patronage into permanent scholarly output.

3.matchText: imperial examinations of Tang and Song China
3.blurb: The civil service examination system that Joseon adopted as gwageo was a direct descendant of the Han-Tang-Song Chinese examinations, with the same Confucian classical curriculum, the same multiple-stage testing structure, and the same theoretical openness combined with practical hereditary capture. The Han dynasty had pioneered the model in 165 BCE; the Tang systematized it; the Song made it the dominant route to office. Joseon's gwageo was the Korean implementation of an institutional pattern over a thousand years old by the time of its adoption.

The Christianization of late-medieval Spain produced, in the Spanish Inquisition, a religious bureaucracy more aggressive than anything in contemporary Italy or France — and the parallel to Joseon's Confucianization is suggestive. New converts often go further than originators. The Joseon yangban's enthusiastic implementation of patrilineal Confucianism (more strictly than China itself) is a case in point. The Byzantine context here is only partial — Spain's Christianization came through Reconquista — but the structural pattern of imported orthodoxy intensified in the receiving culture is widely repeated.

Joseon's nobi system, comparable in proportion (around a third of population) to Ottoman or classical Roman slave populations, raises the same comparative question that the antebellum American South does: what made a society maintain mass slavery for centuries when many neighboring societies had moved to other labor systems? The Ottoman case used military slave-soldiers and household slavery in a different cultural register; Joseon used hereditary domestic and agricultural slavery for its yangban scholar class. Different institutions, similar moral darkness.

While the Joseon yangban order was solidifying in mid-fifteenth-century Korea, the Inca Empire under Pachacuti was at its peak — building the most extensive road network in the pre-Columbian Americas and consolidating an empire of perhaps 12 million people across the Andes. Two highly organized, ritually elaborate, hierarchical state systems building in opposite hemispheres in the same generation, with no contact between them. The early-modern world was being built simultaneously by every civilization that was up to the work.

4.blurb: The Imjin War (1592-98) was contemporary with the Spanish Armada (1588) and represents one of the largest amphibious operations of the sixteenth-century world. Three of the great early-modern naval campaigns — Lepanto in 1571, the Armada in 1588, and Hansan in 1592 — happened within a single generation, demonstrating how sixteenth-century gunpowder warfare was simultaneously transforming naval combat from Constantinople to Manila to the Korean Strait.

Yi Sun-sin's victory at Hansan Island ranks alongside Themistocles at Salamis (480 BCE) as one of the most consequential outnumbered naval victories in human history. Both battles used local geographic knowledge — narrow channels, predictable currents — to neutralize a numerically superior enemy. Both decisively ended a major imperial expansion project (Persian into Greece, Japanese into Korea and beyond). Both became defining moments in their countries' national-identity formation. Yi Sun-sin is often called the Themistocles of Korea, and the comparison is earned.

Hideyoshi's invasion of Korea was the climax of the late-Sengoku unification of Japan that the previous Japanese TL covers — Nobunaga, then Hideyoshi, then Tokugawa Ieyasu, the three great unifiers. Hideyoshi's Korean adventure was, in essence, what he did with the unified Japanese army when he ran out of domestic targets. The war effectively ended Hideyoshi's Toyotomi line and opened the way for Tokugawa Ieyasu to win the Battle of Sekigahara in 1600 and found the shogunate that would govern Japan for the next 264 years.

The Ming intervention in Korea (40,000 troops, 8-25 million ounces of silver) was contemporaneous with the Ottoman wars against the Habsburgs and Safavids — both empires were spending vast sums propping up frontier positions, and both would eventually be exhausted by the cumulative cost. The Ming's Korean expense contributed to the dynasty's fiscal weakening that helped trigger its 1644 collapse; the Ottoman frontier wars contributed similarly to the empire's seventeenth-century stagnation. The cost of holding empires together at their margins is among the great underappreciated factors in early-modern Eurasian history.

The forced transfer of Korean ceramic knowledge to Japan after the Imjin War transformed Japanese pottery for centuries. Korean potters founded the Arita and Satsuma traditions; the Japanese tea-ceremony aesthetic of valuing rough, naturalistic Korean stoneware bowls (the Ido and wabi tradition) emerged in this same period. The cultural cross-pollination was not voluntary — skilled craftsmen were dragged from their homes at swordpoint — but the result was a Japanese ceramic tradition that fed back into international design through the twentieth century.

The Ming intervention in Korea was the largest foreign military operation of the late Ming and arguably the most expensive. The Ming Dynasty TL covers the Imjin War from the Chinese side: the Wanli emperor's decision to defend the tributary, Li Rusong's Pyongyang campaign, the staggering silver outlays that helped trigger the fiscal crisis of the 1620s and 1630s, and the way that the Korean intervention contributed to the Ming collapse a half-century later.

5.blurb: The Byzantine survival in cultural form after political extinction in 1453 — Greek Orthodox monasteries on Mount Athos preserving classical learning under Ottoman rule, the Byzantine literary tradition continuing in diaspora — is a parallel for Joseon's sojunghwa response to the Manchu trauma. Both cultures responded to political defeat by declaring themselves the true preserve of civilization while the visible political order was held by 'barbarians.' Both responses preserved cultural identity against generations of subordination.

Crown Prince Sohyeon's eight-year hostage residence in the Qing court in Mukden and Beijing made him one of the most internationally educated Korean figures of the seventeenth century — and the parallel to other royal hostages in the Mongol-Qing dynastic succession is illuminating. The Qing dynasty had been built on a similar institution of taking children of vassal lineages as hostages and educating them at the Qing court. The Mongol and Manchu pattern of converting captured royal children into intermediaries was an old steppe political technique, here failing because Sohyeon's father refused to accept what the institution had created.

Hamel's thirteen-year detention in Korea is part of the broader story of European traveler-captives accumulating early-modern ethnographic knowledge under non-European confinement. The Ottoman captivity narratives of the same period (Polish, Italian, Russian sailors enslaved in Algiers and Tunis) produced a substantial European literature about the unknown East. Hamel's Journal performed the same function for Korea — producing a detailed European description of an otherwise inaccessible East Asian society from the position of an involuntary insider.

6.blurb: Eighteenth-century Tokugawa Japan was producing its own parallel cultural flowering simultaneously with late Joseon — kabuki theater, ukiyo-e woodblock prints, bunjin literati painting, the Edo school of Confucian thought, the Rangaku 'Dutch learning' tradition that paralleled Korean Silhak in its openness to Western knowledge. The two countries' cultural histories track each other quite closely across the late seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, even as their political systems diverged and their relations remained limited to the Waegwan trading post in Busan.

The Korean Catholic case — a substantial church founded by indigenous lay converts without missionary involvement — is unique in Christian history. The closest parallels are very partial: the spread of Christianity into the early Byzantine countryside often happened through laymen returning from urban centers rather than through formal missionary expeditions, and the Christianization of pre-Constantinian Roman Asia Minor involved substantial lay diffusion. But none of these match the Korean pattern of a deliberate organized lay foundation without any priest present in the country for the first decade.

7.blurb: The First Opium War (1839-42) was the moment Western industrial military power made early-modern Asian state systems definitively obsolete. Joseon's nineteenth-century crisis was happening in parallel — the same Sedo-era institutional decay that left Korea unable to respond to gunboat diplomacy was operating in Qing China at the same time. Both states had highly developed bureaucratic Confucian governance and both were unable to handle the technological and military gap that the Industrial Revolution had opened with the Western powers.

While the Sedo politics were destroying Joseon governance in the early-mid nineteenth century, the Ottoman Empire was attempting the Tanzimat Reforms — a deliberate top-down modernization of the legal system, military, and administration. The Ottoman effort produced more institutional change than anything Joseon managed in the same period, but it ultimately failed to prevent eventual Ottoman dismemberment. The Tanzimat is the model of what Joseon might have attempted but did not — and the Ottoman fate suggests it might not have made the difference even if Joseon had tried.

The Qajar dynasty in Iran was the contemporary of late Joseon — same century, same problem of an early-modern monarchy facing nineteenth-century industrial pressure. The Qajar court was being squeezed between Russian and British imperialism just as Joseon was being squeezed between Russian and Japanese ambition. Both dynasties would lose their independence in the early twentieth century. The structural similarities are obvious; the timing was almost identical; the outcomes were both colonial subordination, in different forms.

8.blurb: The Treaty of Ganghwa in 1876 was a deliberate Japanese imitation of Commodore Perry's 1854 forcing of Japan to open. Japan had spent twenty-two years processing the trauma of Perry's gunboats, modernized rapidly under the Meiji Restoration, and now applied the same playbook to its weaker neighbor. The mimicry is one of the more striking patterns in nineteenth-century East Asian history and shows how a country that has been on the receiving end of imperialism can become its agent within a single generation.

The First Sino-Japanese War (1894-95) marked the end of Chinese suzerainty over Korea after five centuries — the same kind of dynastic-tributary relationship that the Ottomans had maintained over the Balkans for centuries and that was being progressively dissolved by European nationalism in roughly the same period. Both the Chinese tributary system in East Asia and the Ottoman vassalage system in southeastern Europe were collapsing under nineteenth-century pressures of nationalism and Great Power competition. Both collapses produced power vacuums that were filled by aggressive newer states (Japan, Austria-Hungary, Italy).

The Russo-Japanese War of 1904-05 was the first modern war won by an Asian power against a major European power — and the eastern arena of the war (Manchuria, the Korean peninsula, the Yellow Sea) was the same arena where Mongol, Jurchen, Manchu, and Korean armies had fought for centuries. Japan's victory closed the long arc of regional military history by establishing that the most modernized Asian power could now defeat any of the old continental empires that had once dominated this geography.

The Eulsa Treaty of 1905 and the formal annexation of 1910 are covered from the Japanese imperial side in the Meiji Japan narrative — Ito Hirobumi as the architect of the protectorate, the Russo-Japanese War as the geopolitical preparation, and the absorption of Korea as the colonial culmination of forty years of Meiji modernization. The Korean catastrophe is the dark side of the Meiji success story. The same modernization drive that built Japan's modern state machine also built the imperial apparatus that swallowed Korea.

The 1910 annexation is where the Joseon story ends and the Korean Modern story begins. The Korean Modern TL picks up at the colonial moment: thirty-five years of Japanese rule, the cultural-erasure policies, the wartime mobilizations, the comfort-women system, and the long road through liberation, division, war, and the eventual emergence of two very different Koreas on either side of the 38th parallel.
